subtopic_detail: Rate control of new-onset atrial fibrillation without heart failure
stem: You are the general medical resident reviewing a 64-year-old man brought in with palpitations and mild exertional breathlessness over 24 hours. He has hypertension on perindopril. He has no chest pain and no history of heart failure or asthma. He has had no fevers, recent surgery or alcohol binge. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. He is comfortable at rest and the symptoms are not currently distressing.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 132 irregularly irregular, BP 138/86, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Chest clear, JVP not elevated, no peripheral oedema, no murmurs, calves soft
data_table.Investigations: ECG: atrial fibrillation, ventricular rate 130s, no ischaemic changes. TSH 1.8 mIU/L, electrolytes normal, troponin not elevated. Bedside echo: preserved left ventricular ejection fraction. CHA2DS2-VA score 2.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial rate-control agent?

options.text: Start oral digoxin 250 micrograms twice daily for 48 hours then a maintenance dose of 125 micrograms daily as first-line rate control monotherapy in this stable presentation
options.rationale: Digoxin is reserved as second-line rate control or for patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction; beta-blockers are first-line in stable patients with preserved ejection fraction. (Source: NHFA Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines)

options.text: Commence oral metoprolol tartrate 25 mg twice daily and arrange anticoagulation with apixaban 5 mg twice daily for stroke prevention given his CHA2DS2-VA score
options.rationale: A beta-blocker is the first-line rate-control agent for atrial fibrillation in a stable patient without heart failure with reduced ejection fraction or asthma; concurrent direct oral anticoagulation is started because his CHA2DS2-VA score is 2. (Source: NHFA Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for direct current cardioversion under sedation today within 48 hours of estimated onset, without prior anticoagulation, to restore sinus rhythm as the primary management strategy
options.rationale: Even within a 48-hour window, anticoagulation is recommended at the time of cardioversion in patients with an elevated CHA2DS2-VA score and ongoing thereafter. (Source: NHFA Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines)

options.text: Start oral amiodarone 200 mg three times daily for one week and then 200 mg daily ongoing as long-term combined rate and rhythm control in this stable patient with preserved function
options.rationale: Amiodarone is reserved for symptomatic rhythm control in patients failing other rhythm-control agents or with structural heart disease; it is not first-line for stable rate control. (Source: NHFA Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines)

options.text: Commence intravenous verapamil 5 mg over 5 minutes and arrange oral diltiazem maintenance therapy alongside apixaban for ongoing rate control in this clinically stable patient
options.rationale: Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers are second-line and avoided in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction; beta-blockers are preferred first-line in Australia. (Source: NHFA Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines)

explanation.summary: New-onset atrial fibrillation in a stable patient is managed with rate control and stroke risk assessment using CHA2DS2-VA. Beta-blockers are first-line rate-control agents in patients without heart failure with reduced ejection fraction or asthma. Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers are second-line; digoxin is reserved for heart failure or as add-on. A CHA2DS2-VA score of at least 2 in men or at least 3 in women warrants long-term oral anticoagulation with a direct oral anticoagulant.
key_points: Beta-blockers are first-line rate control in stable atrial fibrillation with preserved ejection fraction
CHA2DS2-VA at least 2 in men or at least 3 in women warrants anticoagulation
Direct oral anticoagulants are preferred over warfarin except in mechanical valves or antiphospholipid syndrome
Cardioversion within 48 hours still requires anticoagulation if stroke risk factors are present
Amiodarone is reserved for rhythm control in structural heart disease or refractory cases
explanation.pearls: The CHA2DS2-VA score replaced CHA2DS2-VASc in Australian practice in 2024, removing sex as a stroke risk variable; both men and women are evaluated on the remaining clinical risk factors.

subtopic_detail: Diagnostic workup of suspected pulmonary tuberculosis
stem: You are the infectious diseases registrar reviewing a 41-year-old man who arrived from Vietnam six months ago. He has had a 6-week productive cough with night sweats and 4 kg of weight loss. He has had two episodes of streaks of blood in his sputum. He has no chronic medical conditions and is on no medications. He has had no known sick contacts within his immediate household at present.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 °C, HR 96, BP 122/78, RR 18, SpO2 96% room air
data_table.Examination: Thin man, no clubbing, fine crackles in the right upper zone, no lymphadenopathy, normal cardiac examination
data_table.Investigations: Chest x-ray: right upper lobe cavitating consolidation with fibronodular changes. HIV antibody negative. FBC: Hb 124 g/L, WCC 8.4 x10^9/L, lymphocytes 1.6 x10^9/L. ESR 68 mm/h. CRP 56 mg/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

options.text: Start empirical anti-tuberculosis therapy with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol immediately and obtain microbiological samples after a 14-day clinical response review at the clinic
options.rationale: Empirical treatment before sampling compromises microbiological diagnosis and drug susceptibility testing; airborne isolation and rapid sampling are required first. (Source: NTAC Tuberculosis Management Guideline)

options.text: Place a tuberculin skin test now and proceed to start empirical anti-tuberculosis therapy only if it shows induration above 10 mm at the 48 to 72 hour reading
options.rationale: Tuberculin skin testing assesses latent infection and may be negative in active disease; it does not establish active tuberculosis diagnosis or guide treatment. (Source: NTAC Tuberculosis Management Guideline)

options.text: Arrange a single QuantiFERON-TB Gold-Plus interferon gamma release assay test, place him in airborne isolation pending the assay result and avoid further sputum sampling testing
options.rationale: Interferon gamma release assays detect latent infection and are not diagnostic of active pulmonary tuberculosis; sputum testing for nucleic acid amplification and culture is required. (Source: NTAC Tuberculosis Management Guideline)

options.text: Place him in single-room airborne isolation and collect 3 sputum specimens for acid-fast smear, Xpert MTB/RIF nucleic acid amplification and mycobacterial culture before commencing therapy
options.rationale: Australian guidance requires airborne isolation, three sputum specimens for smear, Xpert MTB/RIF nucleic acid amplification testing and culture; Xpert results within hours allow rapid initiation of treatment with rifampicin resistance information. (Source: NTAC Tuberculosis Management Guideline)

options.text: Order a chest computed tomography scan and bronchoscopy as the first investigation to obtain bronchoalveolar lavage samples for cytology and routine microbiological cultures upfront
options.rationale: Bronchoscopy is reserved for sputum-scarce or sputum-negative cases; sputum sampling is the first-line investigation and avoids unnecessary aerosolisation. (Source: NTAC Tuberculosis Management Guideline)

explanation.summary: Suspected pulmonary tuberculosis is diagnosed by sputum microbiological testing after appropriate airborne infection control. Three early-morning sputum specimens are submitted for acid-fast smear, Xpert MTB/RIF nucleic acid amplification and mycobacterial culture. Xpert provides a rapid result with rifampicin resistance information within hours and is now standard first-line testing in Australia. Treatment with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol is started once microbiological diagnosis is confirmed, ideally with directly observed therapy.
key_points: Airborne isolation in a single negative-pressure room precedes sampling and treatment
Three sputum specimens are submitted for smear, Xpert MTB/RIF and culture
Xpert MTB/RIF provides rapid diagnosis and rifampicin resistance within hours
Tuberculin skin testing and interferon gamma release assays detect latent infection, not active disease
HIV testing is recommended in all newly diagnosed cases of tuberculosis
explanation.pearls: All new diagnoses of active tuberculosis in Australia are notifiable to the relevant state or territory public health authority within 24 hours of suspicion, even before culture confirmation.

subtopic_detail: Massive pulmonary embolism with haemodynamic compromise
stem: You are the emergency department registrar reviewing a 64-year-old woman 8 days after right hip arthroplasty. She has sudden severe breathlessness and pleuritic chest pain. She is on prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg daily. She has no major bleeding risk factors and no recent stroke or surgery to neck or central nervous system. She has not been thrombolysed for any other condition recently.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 124, BP 86/54, RR 32, SpO2 88% on 10 L Hudson mask
data_table.Examination: Pale, cold peripheries, capillary refill 4 seconds, raised jugular venous pulse, clear chest, right calf 4 cm larger than left
data_table.Investigations: CT pulmonary angiogram: bilateral central pulmonary emboli with right ventricular dilation, septal bowing. Troponin elevated. ECG: sinus tachycardia, S1Q3T3 pattern.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Start a continuous enoxaparin infusion at 1 mg/kg twice daily and admit for observation and serial echocardiography to assess for right ventricular recovery over the next 24 to 48 hours
options.rationale: Therapeutic enoxaparin alone is appropriate for haemodynamically stable patients; massive pulmonary embolism with shock requires reperfusion therapy in addition to anticoagulation. (Source: NHFA Pulmonary Embolism Position)

options.text: Insert an inferior vena cava filter via the femoral vein under fluoroscopic guidance as the initial intervention before considering further escalation of anticoagulation or reperfusion
options.rationale: Inferior vena cava filters are considered when anticoagulation is contraindicated; they do not address the existing pulmonary embolism causing shock. (Source: NHFA Pulmonary Embolism Position)

options.text: Resuscitate with intravenous fluids and noradrenaline targeting MAP above 65 mmHg, give intravenous unfractionated heparin and administer alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours
options.rationale: Massive (high-risk) pulmonary embolism with shock requires systemic thrombolysis with alteplase plus heparin and vasopressor support; this regimen reduces mortality in haemodynamically unstable patients. (Source: NHFA Pulmonary Embolism Position)

options.text: Switch to oral apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily and discharge home in 24 hours for outpatient follow-up with general practitioner and physiotherapy
options.rationale: Oral apixaban and outpatient management are appropriate for low-risk pulmonary embolism; massive pulmonary embolism with shock requires inpatient reperfusion therapy. (Source: NHFA Pulmonary Embolism Position)

options.text: Arrange immediate transfer for surgical pulmonary embolectomy as the first-line reperfusion strategy without prior thrombolysis or anticoagulation in this haemodynamically compromised patient
options.rationale: Surgical embolectomy is reserved for thrombolysis failure or contraindication and centres with cardiothoracic capability; systemic thrombolysis is first-line if no absolute contraindication. (Source: NHFA Pulmonary Embolism Position)

explanation.summary: Massive (high-risk) pulmonary embolism is defined by haemodynamic compromise including persistent hypotension or shock. Systemic thrombolysis with alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours plus unfractionated heparin and resuscitation reduces mortality. Submassive (intermediate-risk) pulmonary embolism with right ventricular strain and raised troponin is managed with anticoagulation and close monitoring, with rescue thrombolysis if deterioration occurs. Surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed thrombolysis is reserved for contraindications to systemic thrombolysis or thrombolysis failure.
key_points: Massive PE with hypotension or shock warrants systemic thrombolysis with alteplase 100 mg
Intermediate-risk PE is anticoagulation with monitoring; rescue thrombolysis if deterioration occurs
Low-risk PE may be treated as outpatient with direct oral anticoagulants
Right ventricular dilation and septal bowing are echocardiographic markers of high risk
Surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed therapy is reserved for thrombolysis failure or contraindication
explanation.pearls: Half-dose alteplase 50 mg has been used in catheter-directed thrombolysis trials for intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism with reduced bleeding rates, although mortality benefit remains unproven.

subtopic_detail: Adrenal crisis precipitated by intercurrent illness in a patient on long-term steroids
stem: You are the emergency department registrar reviewing a 52-year-old woman with primary adrenal insufficiency, presenting with 36 hours of vomiting, abdominal pain and progressive drowsiness. She normally takes hydrocortisone 10 mg morning, 5 mg afternoon and fludrocortisone 100 micrograms daily. She has not taken her steroids in the last 24 hours because of vomiting. She has not received intramuscular hydrocortisone at home and is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.2 °C, HR 124, BP 78/46, RR 24, SpO2 97% room air, capillary refill 4 seconds
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, dry mucous membranes, hyperpigmented palmar creases, mild abdominal tenderness, no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: Na 122 mmol/L, K 5.8 mmol/L, glucose 3.0 mmol/L, creatinine 124 micromol/L, urea 14 mmol/L. ABG: pH 7.30, HCO3 18 mmol/L. ECG sinus tachycardia. CRP 84 mg/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer intravenous dexamethasone 8 mg once and arrange transfer to the medical ward for ongoing rehydration with crystalloid fluid resuscitation and broad-spectrum antibiotics
options.rationale: Dexamethasone has no mineralocorticoid activity; hydrocortisone is the agent of choice because it provides both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid effect at high dose. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Triple her oral hydrocortisone dose to 30 mg morning and 15 mg afternoon, give an oral glucose drink and arrange admission for serial monitoring of electrolytes and observation
options.rationale: Oral hydrocortisone is inappropriate when the patient is vomiting and in shock; intravenous administration is required, and triple oral dosing is reserved for milder intercurrent illness. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Restrict fluids to 1 L per 24 hours, give intravenous hypertonic saline 3% for the hyponatraemia and observe for clinical response before commencing any specific steroid therapy
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline and fluid restriction are inappropriate in adrenal crisis with hypovolaemic shock; aggressive isotonic fluid replacement and intravenous hydrocortisone are required first. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Withhold any steroid until a random cortisol level has been processed by the laboratory and the result is available, then dose based on the measured cortisol level result
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis is a clinical diagnosis; treatment must not be delayed for laboratory results because mortality rises rapidly with delay. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Give intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg stat, commence intravenous 0.9% saline with dextrose 5% and start an empirical sepsis workup with broad-spectrum antibiotic cover
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis is a clinical diagnosis treated with stat intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg, aggressive crystalloid resuscitation with dextrose, and treatment of any precipitant such as infection; do not delay for laboratory results. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis is a life-threatening complication of adrenal insufficiency precipitated by intercurrent illness, vomiting or missed steroid doses. Diagnosis is clinical and treatment must not be delayed for laboratory confirmation. Immediate intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg, aggressive crystalloid resuscitation with dextrose to correct hypoglycaemia, and treatment of the precipitant are time-critical. Patients with known adrenal insufficiency should carry a steroid emergency card and have a hydrocortisone autoinjector or intramuscular hydrocortisone available at home.
key_points: Adrenal crisis is a clinical diagnosis; do not delay treatment for cortisol levels
Intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg stat then 50 mg six-hourly or 200 mg over 24 hours infusion
Aggressive crystalloid resuscitation with dextrose 5% corrects volume, sodium and hypoglycaemia
Identify and treat precipitants: infection, surgery, vomiting, missed steroid doses
Hydrocortisone at this dose provides sufficient mineralocorticoid activity, so fludrocortisone is paused
explanation.pearls: Sick-day rules for patients on long-term steroids include doubling the oral dose for fever above 38 °C and tripling for fever above 39 °C, with intramuscular hydrocortisone for vomiting or unable to take oral medications.

subtopic_detail: Discriminating SIADH from hypovolaemic hyponatraemia in a hospitalised patient
stem: You are the general medical registrar reviewing a 72-year-old woman on day 4 of admission with community-acquired pneumonia. She is on oral amoxicillin and clarithromycin. She had been improving but is now mildly confused and has had three falls overnight. She has been drinking freely from the water jug. She has no diuretics and no recent vomiting. She has not received intravenous fluids in the past 24 hours. Her chest is clearing on examination.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 °C, HR 84, BP 124/76, RR 16, SpO2 96% room air, no postural drop documented
data_table.Examination: Moist mucous membranes, no peripheral oedema, JVP not elevated, abdomen soft, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Investigations: Na 122 mmol/L (admission 138), K 4.2 mmol/L, urea 3.4 mmol/L, creatinine 60 micromol/L, glucose 5.4 mmol/L, plasma osmolality 256 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 480 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 56 mmol/L. TSH 1.6 mIU/L. Cortisol 412 nmol/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer 100 mL of 3% hypertonic saline as a single bolus, recheck sodium in 1 hour and limit total correction to 10 mmol/L in 24 hours and 18 mmol/L in 48 hours
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is reserved for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia with seizures or coma; this patient has mild confusion only and free water restriction is the first-line measure. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Commence intravenous 0.9% saline at 100 mL/hour and check the sodium every 4 hours for the first 24 hours and adjust the infusion rate accordingly based on the trajectory
options.rationale: Intravenous isotonic saline in SIADH worsens hyponatraemia because the sodium is excreted while water is retained; free water restriction is the correct approach. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Continue current management without specific sodium intervention and recheck serum sodium and clinical state in 24 hours because the result is likely a pseudohyponatraemia laboratory artefact
options.rationale: Plasma osmolality of 256 mOsm/kg confirms true hyponatraemia; pseudohyponatraemia from severe hyperlipidaemia or hyperproteinaemia is excluded. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Restrict free water to 1 L per 24 hours, cease the clarithromycin which is contributing to free water retention and recheck the sodium in 24 hours after these measures are in place
options.rationale: Urine sodium above 30 mmol/L, urine osmolality above 100, low plasma osmolality, euvolaemia and pneumonia as precipitant confirm SIADH; free water restriction is first-line, and reviewing precipitants is appropriate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Start oral tolvaptan 15 mg daily and recheck the sodium every 6 hours over the next 24 hours, withholding additional treatment if the sodium rises by more than 10 mmol/L in that period
options.rationale: Tolvaptan is a vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist used in chronic or refractory SIADH; it is not first-line and risks overcorrection if used without specialist supervision. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: SIADH is a euvolaemic hypotonic hyponatraemia diagnosed by low plasma osmolality, inappropriately concentrated urine (above 100 mOsm/kg), urine sodium above 30 mmol/L and normal adrenal and thyroid function. Pneumonia, central nervous system disease and many drugs are common precipitants. First-line treatment is fluid restriction to 800 to 1000 mL per day; tolvaptan is reserved for refractory or chronic cases. Correction must not exceed 10 mmol/L in 24 hours and 18 mmol/L in 48 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination.
key_points: SIADH criteria: euvolaemia, plasma osmolality below 275, urine osmolality above 100, urine sodium above 30
Free water restriction to 800 to 1000 mL per day is first-line therapy
Hypertonic saline is reserved for severe symptoms: seizures, coma or severe confusion
Correction limit is 10 mmol/L in 24 hours and 18 mmol/L in 48 hours
Tolvaptan is used in chronic or refractory SIADH under specialist supervision
explanation.pearls: Overcorrection of chronic hyponatraemia at more than 10 mmol/L in 24 hours risks central pontine myelinolysis with locked-in syndrome; if overcorrection occurs, dextrose 5% and desmopressin re-lower the sodium safely.
